questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.subsection: 3.2.1 The Law of Conservation of Mass
questions.structural_anchor: The Law of Conservation of Mass
questions.question: In the reaction between barium nitrate and sodium sulfate, \( \mathrm{Ba(NO_3)_2(aq) + Na_2SO_4(aq) \rightarrow BaSO_4(s) + 2NaNO_3(aq)} \), if the experiment is performed in a closed system, why does the total mass remain unchanged?

options.A: Barium sulfate precipitates, locking mass in place.
options.B: Atoms are neither created nor destroyed; they only rearrange bonds.
options.C: The reaction absorbs heat, which adds mass to the products.
options.D: The white precipitate traps all particles, preventing loss.

questions.hint: Consider what happens to atoms during a chemical reaction.
questions.explanation: The Law of Conservation of Mass states that atoms are neither created nor destroyed. They simply rearrange to form new compounds. Option A describes precipitation but not the fundamental reason for mass conservation.

source_trace.subsection_anchor: 3.2.1 The Law of Conservation of Mass
source_trace.structural_element: The Law of Conservation of Mass
source_trace.content_anchor: mass is neither created nor destroyed in chemical reactions

explanations_tiered.definition: Mass is conserved because atoms are neither created nor destroyed; they simply rearrange.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Chemical reactions involve breaking and forming bonds, but the total number of each atom type remains constant. The same atoms in reactants are present in products.
explanations_tiered.beginner: You have the same LEGO bricks before and after building. You rearrange them into a new shape, but no bricks appear or disappear. That's mass conservation.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: The number of Ba, N, O, Na, and S atoms is identical on both sides of the equation. Atoms are rearranged from reactants to products, not created or destroyed.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Mass conservation follows from conservation of baryon number and lepton number in chemical (non-nuclear) reactions. The reaction equation is balanced, showing atom count invariance.
reasoning_steps: Write the balanced chemical equation.
Count atoms on reactant side.
Count atoms on product side.
Counts are equal — atoms are rearranged, not created or destroyed.
misconceptions: Precipitation does NOT explain mass conservation — it's a physical change, not the fundamental reason.
Heat absorption does NOT change mass — energy has negligible mass in chemical reactions.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Mass conserved → atoms rearranged, not created/destroyed.

glossary_entry.definition: Atoms are neither created nor destroyed in chemical reactions; they are rearranged.

flashcard.front: Why is mass conserved in the reaction Ba(NO₃)₂ + Na₂SO₄ → BaSO₄ + 2NaNO₃?
flashcard.back: Atoms are neither created nor destroyed; they only rearrange bonds.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: In a closed system reaction, why does total mass stay the same?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Atoms are neither created nor destroyed; they only rearrange.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Atoms are conserved in chemical reactions.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Atoms rearrange, not create/destroy → mass conserved.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Connect to balanced equations — same atom count on both sides.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Describe the law of conservation of mass using simple experiments
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Atoms are neither created nor destroyed; bonds rearrange.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.section: 3.1 Historical Development of the Atomic Theories of Matter
questions.question: Who proposed that all materials are made up of four elements: earth, air, water, and fire?
questions.hint: This philosopher lived in the 5th century BC and influenced Plato and Aristotle.
questions.explanation: Empedocles proposed the four-element theory. Plato coined the term 'element' for these substances, but Empedocles originated the concept. Option A (Democritus) proposed atoms, not four elements.

source_trace.section_anchor: 3.1 Historical Development of the Atomic Theories of Matter
source_trace.content_anchor: an ancient Greek philosopher, Empedocles, thought that all materials are made up of four things called elements: earth, air, water, and fire

explanations_tiered.definition: Empedocles proposed the four-element theory: earth, air, fire, and water as fundamental components of matter.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: In the 5th century BC, Empedocles introduced the idea that all matter consists of four basic elements. This theory dominated Western thought for over two millennia.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Empedocles thought everything was made from four ingredients: earth (dirt), air (wind), fire (heat), and water (rain).
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Empedocles' four-element theory (earth, air, fire, water) was later adopted by Plato and Aristotle, shaping chemistry until the 17th century.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Though incorrect, Empedocles' elemental system was an early attempt to reduce matter to fundamental components, influencing alchemy and early chemistry. It persisted until Robert Boyle's The Sceptical Chymist (1661).
reasoning_steps: Recall that Democritus proposed atoms, not four elements.
Identify Empedocles as the originator of the four-element theory.
Plato coined 'element' but Empedocles proposed the four.
misconceptions: Plato coined the term 'element' but did not first propose the four elements — Empedocles did.
Aristotle adopted the theory but did not originate it.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Empedocles → earth, air, fire, water (four elements).

glossary_entry.definition: Empedocles' theory that earth, air, fire, and water compose all matter.

flashcard.front: Who proposed that all matter is made of earth, air, fire, and water?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_deck: Chemistry Unit 3 — History of Atomic Theory
publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Who proposed the four-element theory (earth, air, fire, water)?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Empedocles, a Greek philosopher from the 5th century BC.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Empedocles' theory that all matter consists of earth, air, fire, and water.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: State briefly the history of development of atomic nature of substances
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Empedocles proposed the four-element theory.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: One postulate of Dalton's Atomic Theory states that atoms can neither be created nor destroyed. Which modern discovery modified this understanding?
questions.hint: In ordinary chemical reactions, atoms are conserved, but nuclear processes can change atoms.
questions.explanation: Nuclear reactions can transform one element into another, converting mass to energy (E=mc²). This violates Dalton's 'atoms cannot be destroyed' in nuclear, not chemical, contexts. Option B modifies identical-atoms postulate, not indestructibility.

source_trace.content_anchor: Atoms can neither be created nor destroyed

explanations_tiered.definition: Dalton's postulate of indestructible atoms holds for chemical reactions but fails for nuclear reactions where mass converts to energy.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Nuclear fission (splitting atoms) and fusion (combining atoms) change one element into another, destroying original atoms and creating new ones, releasing energy.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Dalton thought you could never break an atom apart. But in nuclear bombs and the Sun, atoms DO break apart or combine, changing into different atoms.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Dalton's postulate applies to chemical reactions (bond rearrangements). Nuclear reactions involve changes in the nucleus, converting mass to energy (E=mc²) and transforming elements.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Einstein's mass-energy equivalence \(E = \Delta m c^2\) shows mass can be converted to energy. In nuclear fission \(^{235}U + n \rightarrow ^{92}Kr + ^{141}Ba + 3n\), atoms are 'destroyed' and new atoms created, violating Dalton's postulate.
reasoning_steps: Recall Dalton's postulate: atoms cannot be created or destroyed.
Recognize that nuclear reactions change one element into another.
These reactions 'destroy' original atoms and 'create' new ones.
Therefore, nuclear reactions violate Dalton's postulate.
misconceptions: Isotopes modify 'identical atoms' postulate, not 'indestructible atoms'.
Electron discovery modified indivisibility, not indestructibility.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Dalton: atoms indestructible in chemistry. Nuclear reactions: atoms can be destroyed/created.

glossary_entry.definition: Process that changes the nucleus, converting one element to another, violating Dalton's indestructible atom postulate.

flashcard.front: What type of reaction violates Dalton's postulate that atoms cannot be destroyed?
flashcard.back: Nuclear reactions (fission and fusion) destroy atoms and create new ones.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What discovery showed that atoms CAN be destroyed?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Nuclear reactions (fission/fusion) transform elements, destroying original atoms.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Process that changes atomic nuclei, violating Dalton's indestructibility postulate.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Emphasize that Dalton's theory is valid for chemical reactions but not nuclear physics.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Nuclear reactions (fission/fusion) destroy atoms, violating Dalton's postulate.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.section: 3.4 Discovery of Sub-Atomic Particles and the Nucleus
questions.question: In Millikan's oil drop experiment, an oil droplet with an electric charge is suspended motionless when the electric force balances gravity. If the electric field is varied, what did Millikan observe about the charges on the droplets?

options.A: All charges were multiples of a smallest fundamental charge.
options.B: Charges varied continuously with no pattern.
options.C: All droplets had the same charge.

questions.hint: Millikan found that charge is quantized.
questions.explanation: Millikan observed that all measured charges were integer multiples of \(1.59 \times 10^{-19}\) C, proving charge quantization and giving the electron's charge. Option B (continuous) contradicts quantization.

source_trace.section_anchor: 3.4 Discovery of Sub-Atomic Particles and the Nucleus
source_trace.content_anchor: the smallest charge found on the cathode rays was approximately \(1.59 \times 10^{-19}\) coulombs, and the charge on each drop was always an integral multiple of that value

explanations_tiered.definition: Millikan's oil drop experiment showed that electric charge is quantized in integer multiples of the elementary charge \(e\).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: By balancing gravity and electric force on charged oil droplets, Millikan found that all charges were multiples of \(1.59 \times 10^{-19}\) C, the charge of one electron.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Millikan found that droplets could have 1, 2, 3, or more units of charge, but never 1.5 units. This proved charge comes in tiny, indivisible packets.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: The measured charges were always \(e, 2e, 3e, \dots\) where \(e = 1.59 \times 10^{-19}\) C. This demonstrated charge quantization and allowed calculation of electron mass using Thomson's e/m ratio.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Millikan's experiment confirmed the quantization of electric charge: \(q = ne\) where \(n\) is an integer. Combining with Thomson's \(e/m\), electron mass \(m_e = e / (e/m) = 9.11 \times 10^{-31}\) kg.
reasoning_steps: Recall that droplets were suspended when electric force balanced gravity.
Millikan varied the electric field and measured charges.
All charges were integer multiples of a smallest value.
That smallest value is the electron charge.
misconceptions: Charges are quantized (discrete multiples), not continuous.
Not all droplets had the same charge — they had different integer multiples of e.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Millikan: charge is quantized in multiples of e = \(1.6 \times 10^{-19}\) C.

glossary_entry.definition: The principle that electric charge exists in integer multiples of the elementary charge \(e\).

flashcard.front: What did Millikan's oil drop experiment discover about electric charge?
flashcard.back: Charge is quantized — all charges are integer multiples of the electron charge \(e\).

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What did Millikan conclude from his oil drop experiment?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Electric charge is quantized in multiples of the electron charge \(e\).
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Charge exists as integer multiples of a smallest unit \(e\).
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Connect to 'elementary charge' concept — smallest indivisible charge.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Explain the discovery of the electron
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Charges were always integer multiples of a smallest fundamental charge (the electron charge).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.section: 3.4 Discovery of Sub-Atomic Particles and the Nucleus
questions.question: Which property of anode rays depends on the nature of the gas present in the discharge tube?

options.B: They are deflected by electric and magnetic fields.
options.C: Their charge-to-mass ratio (e/m) varies with the gas.

questions.hint: Anode rays are positive ions of the gas in the tube.
questions.explanation: Anode rays are positive ions formed from the gas in the tube. Different gases produce ions with different masses, so the e/m ratio changes. Option A (straight lines) is independent of gas type.

source_trace.section_anchor: 3.4 Discovery of Sub-Atomic Particles and the Nucleus
source_trace.content_anchor: The nature or e/m ratio of anode rays depends upon the nature of gas present in the cathode ray tube

explanations_tiered.definition: Anode rays are positive ions; their charge-to-mass ratio depends on the gas because ion mass varies.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Anode rays (canal rays) are formed by ionization of gas molecules. Hydrogen gives protons (e/m high), other gases give heavier ions (e/m lower).
explanations_tiered.beginner: If you change the gas in the tube from hydrogen to helium, the positive particles become heavier, so their e/m ratio changes.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Anode rays are positive ions \(X^+\). The e/m ratio = e/m_ion. Different gases give different m_ion, so e/m varies. This contrasts with cathode rays (electrons), whose e/m is constant.
explanations_tiered.advanced: For hydrogen gas, anode rays are protons \(H^+\) with e/m = e/m_p. For helium, He⁺ ions have e/m = e/(4m_p), one-quarter the value. Thus e/m ratio identifies the gas.
reasoning_steps: Recall that anode rays are positive gas ions.
Different gases produce ions with different masses.
e/m = charge/mass; mass changes with gas, so e/m changes.
misconceptions: Cathode rays (electrons) have constant e/m regardless of gas.
Straight-line travel and deflection occur for all anode rays but don't depend on gas type.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Anode rays e/m depends on gas mass. Cathode rays e/m constant.

glossary_entry.definition: The ratio of a particle's electric charge to its mass; varies for anode rays with different gases.

flashcard.front: What property of anode rays changes when the gas in the discharge tube is changed?
flashcard.back: Their charge-to-mass ratio (e/m), because different gases produce ions of different masses.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Why does the e/m ratio of anode rays depend on the gas in the tube?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Different gases produce positive ions with different masses.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Ratio of charge to mass; varies for anode rays with gas type.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Anode rays e/m varies with gas (different ion masses).
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Contrast with cathode rays (electrons) whose e/m is constant.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Explain the discovery of the proton
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Different gases give ions of different masses, so e/m ratio changes.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.section: 3.5 Composition of an Atom and the Isotopes
questions.question: What is the correct order for filling electrons into the main energy levels (shells) of an atom?
questions.hint: Electrons fill from the lowest energy level closest to the nucleus outward.
questions.explanation: Electrons fill shells in order of increasing energy: K (n=1, lowest), then L (n=2), then M (n=3), then N (n=4). Option A reverses the order, starting with highest energy.

source_trace.section_anchor: 3.5 Composition of an Atom and the Isotopes
source_trace.content_anchor: Filling energy levels with electrons begins from the lower in energy or the shell closer to the nucleus i.e., K shell and goes on sequentially to L, M, N, etc.

explanations_tiered.definition: Electrons fill atomic shells starting from the lowest energy level (K, n=1) and moving outward.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The Aufbau principle (German for 'building up') states that electrons occupy the lowest available energy level before filling higher levels.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Electrons fill seats closest to the teacher first. K shell is closest to nucleus, then L, then M, then N.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Order of filling: K (n=1, lowest energy) → L (n=2) → M (n=3) → N (n=4). Higher n means higher energy and greater distance from nucleus.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The Aufbau principle derives from quantum mechanics: electrons minimize total energy by occupying the lowest energy orbitals (1s before 2s before 2p before 3s, etc.). Shell order K→L→M→N reflects increasing principal quantum number n.
reasoning_steps: Recall that K shell (n=1) has lowest energy.
L shell (n=2) has higher energy than K.
M shell (n=3) higher than L.
Electrons fill from lowest to highest energy.
misconceptions: Electrons do NOT fill higher shells first — they always fill from lowest energy outward.
K (n=1) is closest to nucleus and fills first, not last.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Filling order: K → L → M → N (lowest to highest energy).

glossary_entry.definition: The rule that electrons fill atomic orbitals from the lowest energy level to the highest.

flashcard.front: What is the correct order for filling electron shells K, L, M, N?
flashcard.back: K → L → M → N (lowest to highest energy).

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What is the correct shell filling order (K, L, M, N)?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: K → L → M → N (from lowest energy to highest).
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Electrons fill lowest energy levels first.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Fill order: K (n=1) → L (n=2) → M (n=3) → N (n=4).
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Reinforce that K is closest to nucleus and has lowest energy.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Write the ground-state electron configurations of atoms using main energy levels
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Electrons fill lowest energy level first: K → L → M → N.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.section: 3.5 Composition of an Atom and the Isotopes
questions.question: Chlorine has two isotopes: Cl-35 (mass 34.97 amu, 75.77% abundance) and Cl-37 (mass 36.97 amu, 24.23% abundance). What is the average atomic mass of chlorine?
questions.hint: Weighted average = Σ(fraction × mass). Use 0.7577 and 0.2423.
questions.explanation: Average = (0.7577 × 34.97) + (0.2423 × 36.97) = 26.50 + 8.95 = 35.45 amu. Option A (35.00) is a simple average ignoring abundance.

source_trace.section_anchor: 3.5 Composition of an Atom and the Isotopes
source_trace.content_anchor: Average Atomic Mass = [(% isotope 1)(mass of isotope 1)] ÷ 100 + [(% isotope 2)(mass of isotope 2)] ÷ 100

explanations_tiered.definition: Average atomic mass is the weighted average of isotope masses using their natural abundances.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Chlorine's average atomic mass (35.45 amu) reflects the 75.77% abundance of Cl-35 and 24.23% abundance of Cl-37.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Chlorine is mostly Cl-35 (lighter) with some Cl-37 (heavier), so the average is closer to 35 than to 37 — about 35.45.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Weighted average: (0.7577 × 34.97) + (0.2423 × 36.97) = 26.50 + 8.95 = 35.45 amu, the value on the periodic table.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The periodic table mass 35.45 amu results from the isotopic mixture. Using exact masses: \( \bar{m} = (0.7577)(34.9689) + (0.2423)(36.9659) = 35.45\) amu.
reasoning_steps: Convert percentages to decimals.
Multiply each isotope mass by its decimal abundance.
Add the products to get weighted average.
misconceptions: Simple average (34.97 + 36.97)/2 = 35.97 amu is wrong because abundances are not equal.
Cl-37 is less abundant, so the average is closer to Cl-35 (35.45), not near Cl-37.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Avg atomic mass = Σ(fraction × mass). Cl: 75.77% Cl-35, 24.23% Cl-37 → 35.45 amu.

glossary_entry.definition: Weighted average of isotope masses based on natural abundance.

flashcard.front: Chlorine: 75.77% Cl-35 (34.97 amu) and 24.23% Cl-37 (36.97 amu). Calculate average atomic mass.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Calculate the average atomic mass of chlorine given 75.77% Cl-35 (34.97 amu) and 24.23% Cl-37 (36.97 amu).
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Weighted average of isotope masses by abundance.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Avg mass = Σ(fraction × mass). Cl = 35.45 amu.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Students often forget to convert percentages to decimals before multiplying.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Calculate the atomic masses of elements that have isotopes

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.section: 3.5 Composition of an Atom and the Isotopes
questions.question: An element has the electron configuration 2, 8, 7. How many valence electrons does it have, and in which shell are they located?

options.A: 2 valence electrons in the K shell
options.B: 7 valence electrons in the M shell
options.C: 8 valence electrons in the L shell
options.D: 7 valence electrons in the L shell

questions.hint: Valence electrons are in the outermost shell — the last number in the configuration.
questions.explanation: Configuration 2,8,7 has outer shell M with 7 electrons. Valence electrons = 7 (group 17 element, chlorine). Option A incorrectly identifies K shell as valence shell.

source_trace.section_anchor: 3.5 Composition of an Atom and the Isotopes
source_trace.content_anchor: electrons that occupy the outermost shell of an atom are called valence electrons

explanations_tiered.definition: Valence electrons are the electrons in the outermost shell; here, 7 electrons in the M shell.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The configuration 2,8,7 means K=2, L=8, M=7. The outermost shell is M (n=3), containing 7 valence electrons.
explanations_tiered.beginner: The last number in 2,8,7 tells you the outer shell electrons. Here, 7 is the valence count, in the third layer (M shell).
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Valence shell is the highest occupied shell = M shell with 7 electrons. This element is chlorine (group 17, halogen).
explanations_tiered.advanced: The valence electrons (n=3, 3s²3p⁵) determine chemical reactivity. With 7 valence electrons, chlorine needs one more to achieve octet, making it highly reactive.
reasoning_steps: Identify the last number in the configuration (7).
Identify the shell associated with that number (M shell).
Valence electrons = number in outermost shell.
misconceptions: Valence electrons are NOT the inner shells (K or L).
8 (L shell) is not valence if M shell is occupied.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Valence electrons = outermost shell count. 2,8,7 → 7 valence electrons (M shell).

glossary_entry.definition: Electrons in the outermost shell; determine chemical properties.

flashcard.front: Configuration 2,8,7 has how many valence electrons and in which shell?
flashcard.back: 7 valence electrons in the M shell (outermost shell).

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: How many valence electrons in configuration 2,8,7?
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Outermost shell M has 7 valence electrons.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.section: 3.4 Discovery of Sub-Atomic Particles and the Nucleus
questions.question: Some scientists initially believed Rutherford's gold foil experiment would show alpha particles passing straight through with little deflection. This incorrect expectation was based on which earlier model of the atom?
questions.hint: The model in which positive charge was spread throughout the atom predicted no large deflections.
questions.explanation: Thomson's model had diffuse positive charge, so alpha particles were expected to pass through with minimal deflection. Rutherford's results contradicted this. Option D (Dalton) also predicted no deflection but Thomson's was the prevailing model at the time.

source_trace.section_anchor: 3.4 Discovery of Sub-Atomic Particles and the Nucleus
source_trace.content_anchor: If Thomson's model of the atom was correct, the positively charged α-particles should smash through the regularly dispersed mass of the gold target like cannonballs

explanations_tiered.definition: Thomson's plum pudding model predicted little to no alpha particle deflection because positive charge was spread throughout the atom.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: In Thomson's model, positive charge was diffuse, not concentrated. Therefore, positively charged alpha particles would not encounter a dense positive center to cause large-angle deflection.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Scientists thought atoms were like blueberry muffins — soft and evenly mixed. They expected alpha bullets to pass right through. Rutherford found a hard seed (nucleus) inside instead.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Thomson's diffuse positive charge would exert weak, widely distributed repulsion on alpha particles, causing minimal deflection. The observed large-angle scattering contradicted this and proved a concentrated nucleus.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The Thomson model predicted small-angle scattering proportional to the number of atoms traversed. Large-angle (\(>90^\circ\)) scattering probability would be negligible. Rutherford's observation of 1 in 8000 backscattered required a point-like, massive nucleus.
reasoning_steps: Recall Thomson's model: positive charge spread throughout atom.
Positively charged alpha particles would experience weak repulsion distributed over large volume.
Thus, large deflections were unexpected.
misconceptions: Bohr's model came AFTER Rutherford, so it could not have been the expectation.
Dalton's solid sphere also predicted no deflection, but Thomson's model was current at the time.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Thomson's diffuse positive charge predicted no large alpha deflection → Rutherford disproved.

glossary_entry.definition: Thomson's atomic model with diffuse positive charge, which predicted minimal alpha particle deflection.

flashcard.front: Which atomic model predicted that alpha particles would pass straight through gold foil without large deflections?
flashcard.back: Thomson's plum pudding model (diffuse positive charge).

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Which model's prediction about alpha particles was contradicted by Rutherford's experiment?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Thomson's plum pudding model (expected no large deflection).
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Thomson's model; predicted minimal alpha scattering.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Thomson model → no large deflection expected. Rutherford → large deflection observed → nucleus.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Highlight the contrast between expectation (Thomson) and observation (Rutherford).
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Explain the discovery of the nucleus
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Thomson's plum pudding model predicted no large deflections.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.section: 3.5 Composition of an Atom and the Isotopes
questions.question: An atom of potassium has 19 protons and 20 neutrons. What is its mass number?
questions.explanation: Mass number = number of protons + number of neutrons = 19 + 20 = 39. Option A (19) is the atomic number. Option B (20) is the neutron number alone.

calculation_steps: A = number of protons + number of neutrons

source_trace.section_anchor: 3.5 Composition of an Atom and the Isotopes
source_trace.content_anchor: Mass number A = (number of protons) + (number of neutrons)

explanations_tiered.definition: Mass number is the sum of protons and neutrons in an atom's nucleus.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Mass number (A) = Z + N. For potassium with 19 protons and 20 neutrons, A = 19 + 20 = 39.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Protons and neutrons are both heavy particles in the nucleus. Add them up: 19 protons + 20 neutrons = 39 total particles (mass number).
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Using \(A = Z + N\): Z = 19 (protons), N = 20 (neutrons), so A = 19 + 20 = 39.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Mass number is the total nucleon count. For \(^{39}_{19}K\), Z=19, N=20, A=39. This isotope is the most abundant naturally occurring potassium.
reasoning_steps: Recall formula: mass number = protons + neutrons.
Substitute values: 19 + 20.
Calculate sum = 39.
misconceptions: Mass number is NOT the atomic number (19).
Mass number is NOT the neutron number alone (20).

glossary_entry.definition: Total number of protons and neutrons in an atomic nucleus.

flashcard.front: Potassium has 19 protons and 20 neutrons. What is its mass number?

explanations_tiered.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Mass number = protons + neutrons = 19 + 20 = 39.
